md: A **quadratic equation** has the form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$ (with $a \neq 0$). 'Solving' means finding the values of $x$ that make it true — its **roots**.
When the polynomial factors nicely, factoring is the fastest way to solve. Here's the trick that makes it work.

blocks.title: The Zero Product Property
blocks.body: If $A \cdot B = 0$, then $A = 0$ or $B = 0$ (or both). The product of two numbers is zero **only when at least one of them is zero**.

md: **1.** Move everything to one side so the equation reads $\text{polynomial} = 0$.
**3.** Set each factor equal to zero.
**5.** Check both answers in the original equation.
Visualize it. The graph below is $y = x^2 + 5x + 6$. The red dots on the x-axis are at $x = -2$ and $x = -3$ — the two solutions:

blocks.title: What 'roots' means geometrically
blocks.body: The roots (solutions) of $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$ are exactly the **x-intercepts** of the parabola $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ — the places where the curve crosses the x-axis.

md: **Step 2.** Factor: need two numbers with product $-10$ and sum $-3$. Pair: $-5, 2$. So $(x - 5)(x + 2) = 0$.
Notice — $x = 0$ is a perfectly valid solution. Don't be afraid of zero.

blocks.title: Limitations
blocks.body: Solving by factoring only works when the polynomial factors *nicely* — with rational, simple roots. For quadratics with irrational or complex roots, factoring is hard or impossible. That's exactly the gap the **quadratic formula** fills (next subtopic).

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Move everything to one side ($= 0$). Factor. Zero product property: set each factor to zero and solve. The roots you find are exactly the x-intercepts of the parabola.
